segments.s520-g25: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نامه is the direct object 'letter'. بخوانی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive ('you read' / 'when you read') used here in a temporal clause. هم‌اندر (literary compound) means 'together/at the same time' and modifies the temporal clause, linking to what follows (i.e. do the following immediately upon reading).
segments.s520-g26: فرستش is فرست + ـش (enclitic 'him') — literally 'send him'; in epic language this functions as an imperative/jussive instruction. به نزدیک ما is a locative phrase 'to near us / to our presence'. شادمان is an adjective 'joyful, rejoicing' describing the state in which he should come (how he is to be sent).
segments.s520-g27: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. بایسته‌ها (plural) = 'the necessary things / necessities' and is the source from which he will be freed. بی‌نیازش is بی‌نیاز + ـش (enclitic 'him') meaning 'make him without need / relieve him of need'. کنم is the first-person singular present/subjunctive 'I will make / I will cause' — together the clause means 'I will make him free from necessities.'
segments.s520-g28: میان یلان is the prepositional phrase 'among the heroes' (میان = among, یلان = heroes). سرفرازش is سرفراز + ـش ('make him s.'): 'make him proud/victorious/honored'. کنَم (here کنَم) repeats the first-person 'I will make' and applies to raising his status among the heroes.
segments.s520-g29: چو is 'when/if'. باشد is the 3rd-person copula ('is' / 'may be') used here in the temporal/conditional clause. به نزدیک is 'near to / close to', and فرزند ما is 'our son/descendant' — together: 'if/when he is near our son'.
segments.s520-g30: نگوییم = ن (negation) + گوییم (1st-person plural subjunctive/present) literally 'we will not say / we do not say.' کو is an interrogative/exclamatory particle 'where / where is' used here in the sense 'that ... is not found' (classical phrasing). نیست is 'is not / does not exist'. پیوند ما 'our bond/relationship' is the subject of نیست. The whole clause says we will not claim that our link is absent.
segments.s520-g31: چو = 'when'. آن نامه = 'that letter'. شاه بابک is the title + name 'King Babak'. بخواند is 3rd-person singular (narrative/past) 'he read' — in epic Persian the prefix ب- often appears with past forms, so this reads 'when King Babak read that letter.'
segments.s520-g32: بسی here is an adverb 'much / a great deal.' خون مژگان is a poetic genitive 'the blood of the eyelashes' (an image for tears/blood collected on the lashes). به رخ = 'upon the face/cheek' (رخ = face/cheek). برفشاند is 3rd-person singular 'he scattered / he threw upon' — literal poetic image: he caused much blood from his lashes to be scattered onto his face.
segments.s520-g17: که introduces a relative/clause ('who/that') describing the subject. شیر is the noun 'lion' (epithet for bravery). ژیانست is a poetic fused form meaning 'is [full of life/force]' or here idiomatically 'is like a lion' — it's a present/stative predicate attached to شیر. هنگام رزم is an adverbial phrase 'at the time of battle' (هنگام + noun), placed after the predicate to mark the circumstance.
segments.s520-g18: به ... ماند is a construction meaning 'to resemble / to be like' (به + noun + ماند). همی is a habitual/emphatic adverb 'indeed/still/always' placed before the time expression; روز بزم is 'the day of the feast/assembly' (روز + بزم). Overall this clause contrasts battle-with-feast behavior.
segments.s520-g19: یکی here functions as 'a certain / one' (indefinite) modifying نامه 'a letter'. بنوشت is the past-tense verb 'wrote' (classical past form of نوشتن) following the object; پس before اردوان gives temporal/seqential sense 'then/afterwards' and names the agent Ardavan (subject) in inverted poetic word order.
segments.s520-g20: سوی + name marks the indirect object/recipient 'to Babak' (direction/toward). نامور and پهلوان are postpositive epithets modifying بابک: 'famed' and 'champion/hero'. Word order is noun + epithets, typical in poetry.
segments.s520-g21: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory form (که + ای) addressing someone 'O...'. مرد is the head noun 'man/person'. بادانش is the fused form of با + دانش 'with knowledge' meaning 'learned' or 'of learning'. و رهنمای links another epithet 'and guide/adviser'. The sequence lists titles/qualities in apposition.
segments.s520-g22: سخن‌گوی here is an epithet meaning 'eloquent / speaker' and is coordinated with preceding epithets by و. با + نام 'with name' = 'of reputation/fame' (با نام). پاکیزه‌رای is a compound adjective 'pure-minded / of pure judgment' placed last to complete the list of virtues.
segments.s520-g23: شنیدم is the first-person past 'I heard' (here 'I have heard [that]'). که introduces the reported clause. فرزند تو is a genitive phrase 'your son' (noun + possessive pronoun), followed by the name اردشیر as apposition (identifying that son).
segments.s520-g24: سواریست is a nominal-predicative phrase 'is a horseman/rider' (است fused as -ست). گوینده 'speaker' and یادگیر 'learner' are predicates in a coordinated series linked by و, all describing اردشیر. Word order places the predicate(s) after the subject, typical of Persian.
segments.s520-g49: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing a list of items. The nouns are coordinated by و 'and' in the normal Persian left-to-right list order (دینار و دیبا و اسپ و رهی). Each noun is a bare noun (no overt case marking) enumerated as gifts/things. رهی here functions as an item related to travel (literally 'for the road' / 'road-related'), completing the series and leading into the next 'ز' phrase.
segments.s520-g50: A second parallel 'ز' phrase continuing the list: ز + noun(s). چینی is used here as the commodity 'Chinese ware / porcelain', then و joins زربفت 'gold-brocade'. شاهنشهی is an attributive/qualifying element ('imperial') that scopes over the preceding item(s). The whole phrase is another set of gifts introduced by the same prepositional marker ز.
segments.s520-g51: بیاورد and بنهاد are two past-tense verbal actions joined by و (a simple narrative sequence: 'he brought and he laid/placed'). پیش takes the sense 'in the presence of / before' and governs the following noun (جوان); here جوان functions as a nominal object (a young man / the youth) who is the addressee/recipient of what was placed.
segments.s520-g52: This clause uses the common Persian copular construction: subject (جوان) + شد (past of شدن, 'became') + predicate nominal (پرستنده اردوان). پرستنده is a deverbal noun/adjective 'worshipper/devotee', and اردوان is the possessor/target introduced directly (no preposition); grammatically this reads 'the youth became a worshipper/devotee of Ardavan.'
segments.s520-g53: بسی modifies هدیه‌ها to indicate abundance ('many' / 'much'); نیز adds the sense 'also' or 'too'. با + personal name (با اردشیر) marks accompaniment/association ('with Ardashir' or 'sent with Ardashir'). Word order is determiner/adjective + noun + particle + prepositional phrase.
segments.s520-g54: Another z-preposition list (ز ...) enumerating the gifts: دیبا و دینار و مشک و عبیر. The nouns are coordinated with و and are direct items of the accompanying gifts. The clause is elliptical (no main verb here), understood as continuing the previous clause describing what the gifts were.
segments.s520-g55: ز پیش forms a locative/origination phrase 'from before / out of the presence of'. نیا here appears as a name or title placed before کودک; کودک is the noun 'child' and نیک‌پی is a postpositive adjective meaning 'well-born' or 'good‑of‑lineage'. The phrase reads as a nominal unit 'from before Niyâ, a well-born child' (subject/topic of the following clause).
segments.s520-g56: به درگاه شاه اردوان is a prepositional phrase 'into/to the court of King Ardavan' (به + noun phrase), functioning as a directional or locative adjunct. شد is the past of شدن 'became' or 'was made', and بری here is a predicative adjective 'free/from' (i.e., 'became free' or 'was freed'). Together they form a clause meaning 'he went into/entered Ardavan's court and became free' (note: classical Persian often places predicate after شد without an additional copula).
segments.s520-g33: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb meaning “he commanded/said.” تا here is the subordinating conjunction “that/so that” introducing the clause. پیش او is a prepositional phrase “in his presence/before him.” شد is a past tense intransitive verb “became/was made” used here to mean “came to be / was placed (as).” دبیر is the complement “secretary.” The order is Verb (honorific) + conjunction + prepositional phrase + past verb + predicate noun: “He ordered that (so that) a secretary be before him.”
segments.s520-g34: This is a noun phrase identifying who the secretary was. همان is a demonstrative “that / that very.” نورسیده is an adjectival participle meaning “newly risen / tender / newly arrived.” جوان is the head noun “young (man / youth).” اردشیر is the proper name placed at the end (typical Persian order: demonstrative/adjectives → noun → name).
segments.s520-g35: بدو گفت = “he said to him.” بدو is the clitic/prepositional phrase “to him,” and گفت is the past verb “said” introducing reported speech. کاین is the archaic fused relative/conjunction ک + این, here functioning as “that/this,” and نامه اردوان is the direct object “the letter of Ardavan.”
segments.s520-g36: This is the reported imperative/command. بخوان is the imperative/present-form verb “read” (or “recite”), and و coordinates the next verb. نگه‌کن is an imperative “keep / hold (for safekeeping).” به marks the beneficiary/recipient “to/for,” and روشن‌روان is the addressee or epithet “Roshan‑ravan” (literally “bright‑souled”). The sequence is two imperative verbs joined by و, then the prepositional phrase indicating with whom/to whom it should be kept.
segments.s520-g37: من اینک sets the subject and time: من “I” and اینک “now.” یکی نامه is “a certain letter” (یکی used as an indefinite determiner). نزدیک شاه here indicates the addressee/recipient “to the king” or “addressed to/for the king” (نزدیک literally “near,” functioning here to locate the letter relative to the shah). The phrase overall sets up the object and its recipient.
segments.s520-g38: نویسم and فرستم are first‑person present forms used here to express immediate action or intent: “I write / will write and I send / will send.” The verbs are coordinated but both refer to the speaker’s planned actions. یکی نیک‌خواه is the indefinite object “a certain well‑wisher” (i.e., a well‑wishing person who will be sent as messenger).
segments.s520-g39: بگویم here is a verb of saying (first person, present/subjunctive flavor “I say / let me say / I would say”), and که is the subordinating conjunction “that.” اینک is the temporal adverb “now.” دل و دیده را is a coordinated definite object “the heart and the eye” marked by را (the Persian definite/direct‑object marker), so this clause introduces what the heart and eye are doing/feeling.
segments.s520-g40: This final segment names who is chosen/favored. دلاور is an adjective/noun “valiant / hero,” جوان specifies “young,” and پسندیده is a past‑participle/adjectival predicate “chosen / approved / pleasing.” The trailing را again marks the chosen one as the definite object; syntactically this segment fills the object slot introduced in the previous segment (the heart and eye have chosen the valiant young man).
segments.s520-g41: فرستادم is a past‑tense, first‑person singular verb ‘I sent’. The conjunction و links two coordinated actions; دادمش is ‘I gave him/it’ with the enclitic ـش indicating the indirect object (him/it). نیز is an adverb ‘also’ modifying the giving, and پند is the direct object ‘advice/counsel.’ Word order is verb (فرستادم), coordinated verb with attached pronoun (دادمش), then adverb and object.
segments.s520-g42: چو آید is a temporal clause ‘when he comes’ (چو = ‘when’ + آید = ‘he comes’); the prepositional prefix ب (= به) attaches to the demonstrative that follows to form بدان = ‘to/into that.’ بارگاه بلند is the noun phrase ‘that lofty court’ with the adjective بلند after the noun (typical in Persian poetry).
segments.s520-g43: تو آن کن is an imperative construction: تو (‘you’) + آن (‘that’) + کن (imperative ‘do’), meaning ‘do that.’ The relative/subordinating که introduces the clause describing what to do: از رسم شاهان سزد = ‘which befits/from the custom of kings’ where از marks the standard or source and سزد is an impersonal/present form ‘it befits / it is fitting.’
segments.s520-g44: نباید is an impersonal negative ‘it should not / one must not.’ The clause introduced by که gives the prohibited situation: بادی (an indefinite ‘a breeze / wind’) برو بر وزد is a subordinate sequence of verbs meaning ‘go and blow upon’ (برو = ‘go’, بر + وزد = ‘upon’ + ‘blow’). Together the clause says that it must not be that even a breeze goes and blows upon you (i.e. do not let even a trifle disturb you).
segments.s520-g45: در گنج بگشاد is a compact verbal clause: در here means 'the door/gate (of)' (not the preposition), گنج refers to the treasure-vault, and بگشاد is the past/archaic perfect 'opened' (گشود). The subject بابک follows the verb (inverted order common in Persian poetry). چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' that attaches to the action, describing how (quickly/gustily) the action occurred.
segments.s520-g46: جوان را is the definite object marked by را ('the young man'). ز هرگونه‌ای is an adverbial phrase 'in/from every kind/way' (ز used poetically to mean 'in/by'), and کرد شاد is a causative-predicative sequence: کرد (caused/made) + شاد (happy). Put together: '(he) made the young man happy in every way.'
segments.s520-g47: This is an enumerative list introduced by the poetic preposition ز ('from/of'): ز زرین ستام 'of golden praise/tribute', و ز گوپال 'and of Gopāl' (proper name or item), و تیغ 'and the sword'. زرین is an adjective 'golden' preceding the noun ستام ('praise/tribute'). The repeated ز gives a rhythmic listing of possessions/gifts.
segments.s520-g48: ز فرزند here means 'as to/for his son' (z used poetically), چیزش 'his thing' (i.e. anything/any possession), and نیامد دریغ is an idiomatic negative: literally 'no reluctance came' → 'he did not withhold (anything)'. The verb نیامد (did not come) plus noun دریغ (reluctance) together express 'there was no reluctance/hesitation (to give)'.
segments.s520-g1: چو ... بگذشت forms a temporal subordinate clause: چو = 'when' introduces the clause and بگذشت is the simple past 'passed' (time passed). بر ماه‌چهر is a prepositional phrase 'over/upon the moon‑faced (one)' modifying the passing of time; word order places the temporal clause at the sentence start (poetic inversion).
segments.s520-g2: یکی کودک آمد is a straightforward past narration 'a certain child came' (یکی here = 'a certain' and آمد = simple past 'came'); پرتابِ simile چـو تابنده مهر follows after the main clause: چو = 'as/like' introduces the simile and تابنده is an adjectival participle 'shining/resplendent'. مهر is used poetically here for the radiant deity/sun (Mithra).
segments.s520-g3: به ماننده = 'in the manner of / like': به is the preposition introducing similarity and ماننده is the comparative 'like'; نامدار اردشیر is the noun phrase 'the renowned Ardashir' serving as the model of comparison.
segments.s520-g4: A sequence of adjectival predicates joined by و: فزاینده (literally 'increasing'—here 'growing/increasing in status or power'), فرخ ('fortunate/auspicious'), دلپذیر ('pleasant/delightful'). They describe the child and follow the comparison introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s520-g5: همان = 'that same' (demonstrative emphasis). اردشیرش is 'Ardashir' with the enclitic -ش referring back to the child (possession/association); پدر کرد is a compound where کرد ('made/did') combines with پدر ('father') to mean '(was) made father' or 'became father' in this poetic causative/active phrasing. نام at the end begins the next phrase 'named ...' (name + شد is split across segments).
segments.s520-g6: نیا شد = 'became Niyâ / was named Niyâ' (نام from the previous segment combines with نیا شد to yield 'was named Niyâ'); به دیدار او is an adverbial phrase 'at/on seeing him' or 'at the sight of him' (به + noun), and شادکام is a predicate adjective 'joyful/fortunate' governed by شد ('became'). The verb شد is the simple past 'became'.
segments.s520-g7: همی is a repetitive/adverbial particle 'continually/always' that gives a habitual sense to the past verb. پروریدش is the simple past 'nurtured/raised him' with the object enclitic -ش. به بربر indicates the people/place 'among / with the barbarians' and به ناز gives the manner 'with pampering/affection'—both are adverbial complements of the verb.
segments.s520-g8: برآمد = 'arose/occurred/was brought about' (simple past). برین is a fused/poetic locution meaning 'thereupon / on this' (بر + این/ین), and روزگاری دراز is the noun phrase 'a long period of time' that serves as the subject/complement of برآمد; the whole phrase means 'thereupon a long time came about' (i.e. a long period ensued).
segments.s520-g9: This initial phrase names the person and topicalizes him. او را is the direct object 'him' (او = 'he', را = object marker). کنون 'now' gives time, and مردم 'the people' is the (implied) subject of the following verb. مر is an archaic/contracted element here (see senses): it functions as an emphatic or dative-ethical particle and does not alter the core object–subject relation.
segments.s520-g10: همی before the verb gives a habitual/continuous sense ('would/used to' or 'still/always'). خواندش is the past-form verb with the enclitic object -ش 'him' (here meaning 'called/named him'). The proper name بابکان اردشیر (Babakān Ardashīr) follows the verb in the V‑S‑O/archaic poetic order and is the agent: 'Ardashīr Babakān called him (thus)'.
segments.s520-g11: بیاموختندش is a past plural verb 'they taught' with the object enclitic -ش 'him' attached. هنر هرچ بود is a nominal clause 'whatever art there was' (هرچ = 'whatever', بود = past 'was'), functioning as the direct object/what they taught.
segments.s520-g12: This clause states the effect/result. هنر نیز 'art also' (نیز = 'also') is the topic; بر + گوهرش + بر فزود is a classical construction where بر + verb (فزود) means 'added upon' or 'increased'. گوهرش ('his essence/inner worth') is the indirect object/target of the augmentation, and فزود is a past/poetic perfect meaning 'it increased/added'.
segments.s520-g13: چنان شد is 'he became such' (چنان = 'so/such', شد = past 'became'). The preposition به introduces the domains: به دیدار و فرهنگ و چهر = 'in appearance, in refinement/manners, and in countenance' (a tricolumn listing qualities in which he transformed). Word order is adjective/adverbial phrase after the verb, typical of epic diction.
segments.s520-g14: که introduces the result clause 'that ...'. گفتی here is a poetical reference to 'as you said' or 'as it has been said' (an elliptic usage in the text). همی again gives a continual/emphatic tone. ز + و (here contracted as 'زو' in older Persian) = 'from him' and فروزد سپهر is verb + subject inversion common in poetry: فروزد 'shone/kindled' and سپهر 'the sky/heavens' is the thing that shone (i.e., the heavens were lit by him).
segments.s520-g15: پس 'then/thereupon' sets the sequence. آگاهی آمد is a common pattern 'news/information came' (آگاهی used as 'information' here). سوی اردوان 'to Ardavān' uses سوی + proper name to indicate direction/recipient: 'reached Ardavān'.
segments.s520-g16: This prepositional phrase specifies the content of the news: ز فرهنگ وز دانش آن جوان = 'concerning the manners and the learning of that young man.' Here ز = 'of/about/from', وز is an older contraction (و + ز) linking the two nouns 'فرهنگ' and 'دانش' ('and of' / 'and'), and آن جوان = 'that young man.'

sentences.s520-s13: When you read the letter, at the same time send him to our presence, rejoicing.
sentences.s520-s14: I will free him from the necessities, and I will make him honored among the heroes.
sentences.s520-s15: If he is near our son, we will not say that our bond does not exist.
sentences.s520-s16: When King Babak read that letter, he scattered much blood of his lashes upon his cheek.
sentences.s520-s9: who is a lion in battle, and on the day of the feast he resembles Nahid (Anahita).
sentences.s520-s10: Then Ardavan wrote a letter to Babak, the famed champion.
sentences.s520-s11: O man of learning and guide, eloquent and of good name and pure-minded.
sentences.s520-s12: I have heard that your son Ardashir is a horseman, a speaker, and a learner.
sentences.s520-s25: Of dinars and brocade, of steeds and travel-gear; of Chinese wares and imperial gold-brocade.
sentences.s520-s26: He brought them and placed them before the youth; the youth became a devotee of Ardavan.
sentences.s520-s27: Many gifts also accompanied Ardashir: brocade, dinars, musk and perfume.
sentences.s520-s28: From before Niyâ, a well-born child; he entered the court of King Ardavan and became free.
sentences.s520-s17: He commanded that a secretary be brought before him — that same newly arrived youth, Ardashir.
sentences.s520-s18: He said to him, “Read this letter of Ardavan, and keep it with Roshan‑ravan.”
sentences.s520-s19: I will now write a letter to the king and send a certain well‑wisher (as messenger).
sentences.s520-s20: I say that now the heart and the eye have chosen the valiant young man.
sentences.s520-s21: I sent it and also gave him counsel when he came to that lofty court.
sentences.s520-s22: Do that which is fitting according to the customs of kings; one should not allow even a breeze to go and blow upon (you).
sentences.s520-s23: When Babak opened the treasure, like the wind he made the young man happy in every way.
sentences.s520-s24: He withheld nothing from his son — neither golden praise, nor (Gopāl), nor the sword.
sentences.s520-s1: When nine months had passed over the moon‑faced woman, a child came forth like the radiant Mithra (the shining sun).
sentences.s520-s2: Like the famed Ardashir—growing, fortunate, and delightful.
sentences.s520-s3: That same Ardashir became his father; he was named Niyâ, and at seeing him he was joyful.
sentences.s520-s4: They continually raised him among the barbarians with indulgent care; thereafter a long period ensued.
sentences.s520-s5: Now the people call him Tizvīr; Ardashīr Babakān used to call him so.
sentences.s520-s6: They taught him every art there was; art, too, increased his inner worth.
sentences.s520-s7: He became such in looks, manners, and countenance that, as you said, the heavens shone from him.
sentences.s520-s8: Then news came to Ardavān of that young man's manners and learning.
